speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January 17, 1997. The survivor is Harry Konar, K-O-N-A-R. We're in Riviera Beach, Florida, and this is tape number three.

speaker: interviewee
text: One little thing that happened, a way that my brother in law's brother who was shipped back from Germany-- from Leipzig to Radom-- before the war was an instance I walked with him. And one of the Germans, a single-- I don't even know what the rank he was. I don't even think he was an SS-- tripped me. He saw a Jew walking. And how he recognized me and so on-- he showed me a trick. He says, oh, let's carry--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who showed you the trick?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother in law's brother, who was from Germany. He says, carry a German newspaper with you always when you go. And make believe you're reading it and so on, so they won't bother you. I did carry it, and it worked. Going back to the ghetto, and after, after they shipped everybody out, and after we came back when the Russians escaped, they took us back into the small ghetto. We were fortunate, because some of the groups that they recruited within the same two weeks of ours-- one of them especially. We almost went there. They were about 40 people. And this was a special group. And their only purpose was-- they knew then in '44 that they lost the war. Their only purpose was that they uncovered the graves of people in Radom, mostly Jewish people, especially one near the hospital. And they burned the corpses. And then they were killed. And this was going on. This was a job that took--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews. They killed them all to show them--

speaker: interviewer
text: The ones who were burning the corpses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. It was a special commando established by the SS. And they didn't want to leave any traces of bodies. So wherever-- they must have had records. They were very thorough, the Germans. They had records of everything. And there was a grave, I understand, one of them with quite a few people or maybe a thousand that they just shot-- people who couldn't walk to the train station, people who couldn't-- when they liquidate them or whatever happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't see this.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard.

speaker: interviewee
text: But yeah, I know what happened because all my 40-- almost 40-- the 40 of them were from the same area, and all of them died in one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were from a group that was near you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah. As a matter of fact, the officer who came to the supply house to get food for them brought me regards from so and so, you know? And I would ask him-- once you get closer to them, they'd talk sometime. Not that he was a friend of mine, but I told him tell so and so that I asked for him. And he would come back the next week and say, so he's doing good. I'm sure he did because they were very friendly to me because I was in the position where I distributed them food and their wine, their liquor, their everything. So I was accepted.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know what they did with the bodies of the Jews they killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 40? No, I don't know. I don't know. I know they were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: He came-- the reason I know-- we found out later they killed him. But I knew then when he came back for the food for his group for the SS. And I says you're, not getting enough today, you know? I don't need it anymore. So we had the idea that they were liquidated.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Then one day, there was, in '44-- also in the summertime-- one of our-- there was-- we were a small group in there, maybe 15 or 20 people. One Jewish man escaped. And it didn't take long for them to come down from the concentration camp, from the ammunition factory, gather us all up. No, no this. I was called up from work at 12 o'clock, asked to come outside, and marched into the-- into the concentration camp without connection with the ammunition factory. We stayed there about approximately two months or less.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a special name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Konzentrationslager. That was all it was in Radom, you know. Oh, there were quite a few thousand people-- maybe 4,000 or 5,000 because it was the main ammunition factory that supplied. But like I said, like everybody else knew the war was lost. We knew everything coming back from the Russian front and the Russians were not too far from us anymore, in Lublin or so. They couldn't even get a train for us so they marched us for thee days to a city called Tomaszow. In the meantime, anybody who couldn't walk anymore was put on a wagon. And they took him to the forest and shot. We heard the shots. We didn't see it, but we knew-- people that I knew from home. You know, I can give you names of some of them. We came into a-- but they told us that we're going to work in a factory near, near Stuttgart or so on. And we figured that the officers of the camp and so on are trying to hide their own skin that they don't have to go into the Russian front, and build a factory to keep themselves busy. Because it's-- it looked like this is what they were trying to do already this late in the game. We came in there, and it wasn't bad. I did not work at the factory. One of my brothers did. I was assigned to the kitchen because the chef of the kitchen knew me. So he picked me out in the first. Conditions were just like in any concentration camp. Food was not enough unless you worked on the kitchen-- in the kitchen or you can help yourself

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the chef German or Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, German. No, no, all German. Now, my younger brother went into work in the kitchen in the camp. There, the chef was German too, but the assistant chef was Jewish, a neighbor of ours. The fortunate thing was that we stayed together, that we stayed in Radom so long together. Then it was easier. In this camp I was for about approximately from August until November or so, approximately. And I was transferred out to another camp, Unterriexingen. And this is where I pushed the planes and so on. Other people did some other things. The conditions were much worse in Unterriexingen. There was no food to be had. But fortunately, whenever we had to go for supplies to the Vaihingen camp. So I always volunteered for this even so if it meant-- there were no horses anymore-- pushing the wagon and walk 10 to 15 kilometers. But I was still strong to do it. And once I got to Vaihingen, my brothers could help me, and I got some extra food.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Vaihingen?

speaker: interviewee
text: V-A-H-- oh, V-A-I-H-I-N-G-E-N, which is near Stuttgart. Things were bad. As a matter of fact, I lost one of my cousins, Sam's brother. There wasn't enough food to go around and the work was hard, even on the last months of the war. Then I think in November or December, they liquidated this camp. And I was one of 10 or 12 people that marched us back to Vaihingen.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Unterriexingen?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Unterriexingen, yeah. When I came back there, we still worked. But most of them-- at this point, most of them were sick people, dying people. You know where they were dying.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was one or two barracks especially where you were-- first of all, they didn't have-- there were Jewish doctors. But if you didn't have no medicine, what could they do? No control. But they put you in there to die in those barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: I managed to work at the hospital. They work every day went out until we knew the liberation was coming. We knew we were going to be liberated if we survive. The ones that when on the march two days before we were liberated-- they got a few people together from the camp to, to go to the train station, go out. But they left 12 people to work in the kitchen. And they left my two brothers. My younger brother took my other one in in the meantime. So when I saw this-- I don't know how I did it-- I turned away from the group and went into the kitchen too. They start counting and counting. They had 13 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I run into the barrack. There was a Jewish doctor in there. As a matter of fact, his son is a professor at Cornell University-- or was. And [INAUDIBLE] told him what I'm doing and so on. He put me up on the top bunk, put some disease on it. And nobody touched me for 24 hours until we were liberated-- a miracle. [SOB]

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to go a march, you know, because we knew they went to-- on the march they took, most of the people died. Any of the marches, they died. And I figured, I'll stay. They didn't recognize me because we all looked alike. And the Jewish men would have turned-- he had to turn me in. But he didn't know which corner I was left. And that's-- I would not--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who didn't turn you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish kapo still there. I went outside, trying to hide outside. And there was still a guard stationed around the camp. And when he saw me, he went for the gun like this. I run fast back in, but I run into the hospital block, which was my savior.

speaker: interviewer
text: They let the doctor stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they let the doctor stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other people in the hospital section?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the, the block manager, which was Jewish from my hometown. See, the people knew us, so it was-- they looked for you, or tried to protect us as much as they could. It wasn't up to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why they left those men there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They needed some supervision, you know. I mean, they didn't leave too many. Maybe a block with 400 people, they left one of two men, just to see that they give them the food or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these sick people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, dying people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, dying people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dying people, they were not sick. I mean, strictly, if you were able to walk, you walked. They were strictly dying people. And then after we were liberated, they took--

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: April 8, 1945. They took us-- again, we were quarantined.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The French. They emptied out the village not far from Vaihingen, and they put us in there for a few weeks to give us food. And the best thing we got was a shower. I hadn't showered in I don't know how long, you know? And they gave us the food. And then the Americans have occupied more of the German country, and they immediately signed the Armistice agreement. We were transferred then to the American, American side. And that's where we ended up in [NON-ENGLISH]. We stayed in [NON-ENGLISH] for about two or three months. In the meantime, I found out that my cousin's alive, Sam, in, in Munich in Feldafing, a concentration camp. So I went down there. In those days, the trains were not running, just freight trains. I hopped a train and went there and brought him back to our place, which was much nicer. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your physical condition when you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mine was good. My brother-- one of my brothers came down with typhus. And he had to go into the hospital two days after liberation, or three days-- something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you of a natural, normal weight?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No, but I was healthy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did weigh, you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I weighed about 120 pounds, which was good for those days-- skinny. I think I'll show you a picture from what I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once we came in-- then after two, three months, we went out from whatever it was. From [NON-ENGLISH], they took us to Stuttgart. And General Eisenhower came down to the camps. He saw what happened. He got touched. He emptied out an apartment complex which used to be a Jewish section in Stuttgart West, and we were moved in there. We were assigned apartments. We had-- our apartment was four or five people-- a two bedroom apartment, which was palatial. We had a kitchen. We had our own food. We had our own kitchen, too. They took a, a restaurant from nearby. And they supplied the food and cooked and so on. And we went in there to eat every day. And if you managed on your own something, you could cook in your own house if you had it. Then my youngest brother-- this is when the United Nations Relief Agency seeked out the people 13 or 14 years younger and put them into a school which was called a Aglasterhausen. In beginning of '46, early '46, they took them all to the United States, just those young people. We, in '46-- this man who was liberated with me, he had a leather factory. And he knew the family. As a matter of fact during the war, I think I mentioned, he lived with my aunt and cousins and so on. He said, Harry, come with me. And he-- since this man Freudenberg came to his factory and took away the leather or so on, he went back to him. And he wanted some compensation, something. Well, the Freudenbergs were more civilized than a lot of other Germans. Even so, from what I understand, they took over several Jewish factories of leather in Germany and paid a pittance for it, but they later made up for it or so on. He said, we'll help you. There's a man here. He's a German, but he worked for the Jewish leather factory. And he also was punished a little bit so he didn't have a job during the war. And because he worked for Jews and stood up for them, he was an outcast in the small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they got them both two together, Mr. Stein. And they opened up an agency for them, a franchise to sell leather wholesale in Stuttgart. And this was established in '46. By the time we opened it up, it was the end of '46 because we had to-- everything was bombed out. You couldn't find anything. So I went with him. Do you remember that man's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Pinkus Hoffner. He's dead now.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, the man who, who was punished lightly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah-- Stein. I don't remember his first name, but he lived in Weinheim in Germany. He's long gone. He died about 20 years ago, 25 years ago. I knew his children because one of his daughters and son-in-law worked with me in the office. And we opened up the store and everything was going OK. But I just couldn't stay in Germany. That was not for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: From beginning when we were liberated, we had different ideas. When we were camps, we-- there were times we thought we'd never go back to Judaism. Very honest with you. A lot of us talked. I mean, why suffer? What, what for? Nobody saved us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we thought we'd go back to Poland and start over. Then we heard what was going on in Poland. The Jews came back, and they were killed, some of them. My cousin went back to reclaim some office, and he was jailed. His mother finally saved him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sam Konar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was bailed out and came, came running back to Germany. Where are you going to go? There was no place to go. And Germany, I wouldn't stay. The German people-- I got to admit this-- are different than the Polish people. I felt freer walking in Germany after the war than I did after going back to Poland or Lithuania. I just didn't feel right there. We went in a group of us, from the Holocaust in Lithuania to a restaurant for lunch. We had a big meeting there. And I went down to use the facilities, and they were singing anti-Semitic songs.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: '92, you know? This doesn't happen in Germany, you know? What it is, they're more cultured or whatever it is. I don't know why they don't do it. They probably do it to the-- thinking of it themselves, but never have I heard a bad word. I was welcome wherever I went and whatever. But not in Poland, and not in Lithuania. Ukraine, I understand, is even worse. I've never been there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there. Then my other brother left in '48 for the United States. You know, it was hard to-- because we didn't have any family. Yes, we did have family here, but I didn't know where they were. Because I can remember my grandmother receiving a few dollars or whatever it was-- I don't know-- or maybe just a card from the United States every now and then. And we were told then that she had family here. But I didn't know who to write to, or I didn't know who to talk to. And the quota was limited. They only allowed so many.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you talk to different people, I don't know. You'll hear different things. There was no quota for a lot of Germans. There was not quota for a lot of Lithuanians or Estonians or whatever have you, the killers, but there were a big quota for us, you know? I don't have no basis. I'm only saying this from things that I've heard. A little quota for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a little quota. And we couldn't-- they took my brother from '46 when-- here to get him down there. In '48, he had to get somebody in Rochester to sign for us. And then I left in '49. My cousin, meantime, had another family established. He's the one who got married in '33 and had a wife and child he lost. When he went back to Poland, he met somebody from France and so on, and they married. In the meantime, he had another child. He left in '48 to Milwaukee.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived in America when?

speaker: interviewee
text: May of '49. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] All of our three brothers then got an apartment for ourselves. We settled down. I came down in May. In June, I had a job working in a warehouse-- $0.80 an hour. It was beautiful. You know, we didn't care about anything. I stayed with the same company for about 10, 11 years. But my brother-- the youngest one who came here early and he graduated in '48 from high school then went to college for a year or two, part time-- became a Hunt's food salesman. And he was exposed to the retail and so on. And in the early '50s, he opened up his own business-- wholesale, at first, pickles and whatever to sell Hunt's products and other stuff. Then later, he came across-- somebody opened up health and beauty aid stores. This was quite a few years later, maybe '56, '58.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came to me since I was then manager. I had made the top in this field. I don't know what top was, you know, but I had a big store to manage and so on, with a lot of people to supervise. And he asked me if I would help him, and he had started it all by himself.

speaker: interviewee
text: My other brother did work for him. So it took me about two or three years. I didn't want to go because first of all, he couldn't afford to pay me what I was making. He was still small. But I stayed on and helped him out mornings and nights whenever I could because I worked shifts in the stores. They were open 9:00

speaker: interviewee
text: 00. And I either had morning stuff or night stuff. So we worked together-- you know, little by little, not rushed. And it took me about-- in 1960 or '61, I went with him. And by then, he was established. And we built up quite a few stores, were very happy. Can't complain about life, definitely not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I married in '54. My other-- my middle brother met a woman here-- a girl here with her mother from Lodz, from another town. And they ended up in the same camp as ours in Stuttgart. I don't know why because it was strictly and originally only from our home town. But a lot of people came in. She came here about the same time or maybe six months earlier into Rochester. And he knew her from before. He used to go out with her. So they got married in 1950. And my other brother and I were married within two weeks of each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have two boys. One is married. Two grandchildren. And life goes on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you retire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I retired in '86. The company that my brother had was sold in 1973-- '72 or '73. And then I started something on my own, a few stores. And I took my oldest son into it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I decided and-- I bought this place here in '82 because life is short, and to go on and work all your life not having anything to show for it. So we came down here and bought it. And came down here in the winter time for a few months and still went back there for Christmas time and so on to help him out. But after, oh, approximately 12 or 15 years since the company was sold in '91 and '92-- in '91, I think it was-- he decided he didn't want to retail anymore. Retail became rough, especially in clothing. It's a hard life. The stores now are open 9;00 to 9:00 or whatever, seven days a week, where in my time when I managed a store, except weekends, Friday and Saturday, 9:00 to 6:00, that was it. Today, it's-- and he wanted a different life. His wife was a reporter for the newspaper for many years. And he liquidated the stores. And he became a stock broker.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you returned to your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where, in Poland?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went through with the Holocaust Museum after they opened it up. They decided to take-- there were about 30 or 40 people for a tour to go back. And I think I mentioned to you, mostly they were going to bring back whatever was left from the graves-- bones and so on-- for the memorial. But we visited the hometown. Our home was bombed out. There was nothing there. Radom, which had 30,000 Jews-- you know, I've got to correct myself. Maybe it was 30,000 people, not 30,000 families. I don't-- you know. I don't think there's one left there. Nobody. There, I drove around and I had a driver who happened to be a Jewish man who was liberated of Auschwitz. But he was a communist from before the war, and he had a Polish girlfriend. He went back to Warsaw, married her, and they're still living there. I've got his name too, you know. It was given to me by a friend of mine. It's a small world, you never--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you know Roman Kent? He's very involved-- [? Eisner-- ?] I have-- [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You never know who you're going to run into. I went with a cousin of mine, an adopted cousin who we were born in the same town-- we went to Radom. There was nothing there to look at. We went back to the town I was born. The house is still there. I have pictures of it. But there isn't a Jew in town. The cemetery is gone. The beis medrash is gone-- just like it never happened. The town has changed-- beautiful homes, you know, from what I remember. The streets are paved. In my day, the streets were not paved, except the main square and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you would like to add to what you've said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm glad I did it. I'll tell you one thing that happened, and it's also not easy to talk about. After I came back from Poland, my youngest son who had trouble-- who has problems, he was being taken care of just a few years ago. We try everything we can do for him, you know-- a psychiatric doctor.
